Blige"),[[409]](#cite_note-410) [Selena](/wiki/Selena "Selena"),[[410]](#cite_note-411) [Anita Baker](/wiki/Anita_Baker "Anita Baker"),[[411]](#cite_note-a870-412) and [Toni Braxton](/wiki/Toni_Braxton "Toni Braxton").[[412]](#cite_note-Beyonce's_Inspirations-413)[[413]](#cite_note-n861-414) She has cited [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama")—44th [First Lady of the US](/wiki/First_Lady_of_the_US "First Lady of the US")—as a personal inspiration.[[414]](#cite_note-m301-415)[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodriguesPrzybylo2018339-416) She described [Oprah Winfrey](/wiki/Oprah_Winfrey "Oprah Winfrey") as "the definition of inspiration and a strong woman".[[411]](#cite_note-a870-412) Beyoncé has stated that her husband, rapper Jay-Z, inspires her, and she has praised his lyrical talent and the challenges he has overcome.[[416]](#cite_note-417) She expressed admiration for artist [Jean-Michel Basquiat](/wiki/Jean-Michel_Basquiat "Jean-Michel Basquiat"), stating that she wants to emulate his lyrical and raw artistic traits in her music.[[417]](#cite_note-418) Beyoncé has named Ross and [Cher](/wiki/Cher "Cher") as some of her fashion inspirations.[[418]](#cite_note-z782-419)

### Videography and stage

[![A woman on stage wearing a structural black-and-yellow bodysuit resembling a bumblebee](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Tottenham_Hotspur_Stadium_-_1st_June_2023_%28104_of_118%29_%2852946039919%29_%28face_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Tottenham_Hotspur_Stadium_-_1st_June_2023_%28104_of_118%29_%2852946039919%29_%28face_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Tottenham_Hotspur_Stadium_-_1st_June_2023_(104_of_118)_(52946039919)_(face_cropped).jpg)

[![A woman on stage wearing a colorful outfit and black sunglasses](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Tottenham_Hotspur_Stadium_-_1st_June_2023_%2838_of_118%29_%2852946363558%29_%28face_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Tottenham_Hotspur_Stadium_-_1st_June_2023_%2838_of_118%29_%2852946363558%29_%28face_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Tottenham_Hotspur_Stadium_-_1st_June_2023_(38_of_118)_(52946363558)_(face_cropped).jpg)

Beyoncé featured 148 different styles of clothing during the [Renaissance World Tour](/wiki/Renaissance_World_Tour "Renaissance World Tour") (examples pictured).[[419]](#cite_note-n001-420)

Beyoncé's music videos are known for their visual storytelling, frequent choreography, and thematic cohesion.[[420]](#cite_note-421) Each track on the albums *Beyoncé* (2013) and *Lemonade* (2016) pairs with a music video to create a continuous narrative, a format known as the "visual album" that she pioneered.[[182]](#cite_note-r354-183)

Beyoncé has worked with numerous directors for her music videos, such as [Melina Matsoukas](/wiki/Melina_Matsoukas "Melina Matsoukas"),[[421]](#cite_note-j239-422) [Jonas Åkerlund](/wiki/Jonas_%C3%85kerlund "Jonas Åkerlund"),[[422]](#cite_note-u349-423) and [Jake Nava](/wiki/Jake_Nava "Jake Nava").[[423]](#cite_note-e477-424) Beyoncé has directed several of her own films, including the musical film *Lemonade* and the documentary concert film *Renaissance: A Film by Beyoncé*.[[424]](#cite_note-o369-425)[[425]](#cite_note-d028-426) Scholar [Salamishah Tillet](/wiki/Salamishah_Tillet "Salamishah Tillet") has observed that Beyoncé's creative control across production roles, visual and thematic style, and personal, cultural, and artistic influences establish her as an [auteur](/wiki/Auteur "Auteur").[[424]](#cite_note-o369-425) A *[Vulture](/wiki/Vulture_(website) "Vulture (website)")* review of *Lemonade* described Beyoncé as a "brilliant filmmaker" whose [allegorical](/wiki/Allegorical "Allegorical") style, characterized by its symbolic visuals and poetic narrative, transcends conventional music videos.[[426]](#cite_note-y505-427)

Beyoncé is known for her meticulously produced and physically demanding performances that incorporate elaborate costumes, striking visuals, and stage design.[[427]](#cite_note-428) Her shows are characterized by large-scale production elements such as LED staging, supporting ensembles, theatrical props, and numerous costume changes.[[428]](#cite_note-z726-429) When performing, Beyoncé uses different fashion styles that coordinate with the music she is singing.[[429]](#cite_note-Wearingmydress-430) According to *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, Beyoncé is the most in-charge female artist onstage,[[430]](#cite_note-j572-431) while *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* wrote she "takes her role as entertainer so seriously she's almost too good".[[431]](#cite_note-i413-432) Critics have credited her with reinventing the festival set and commended her delivery of performances under adverse conditions.[[432]](#cite_note-s538-433)[[433]](#cite_note-r106-434)

Having garnered critical acclaim for her voice and stage presence during concerts,[[434]](#cite_note-435) Beyoncé is often regarded as one of the greatest performers of all time.[[435]](#cite_note-u947-436)[[436]](#cite_note-i047-437)[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones2025154-438) She created the alter ego Sasha Fierce to separate her stage persona from her personal attributes. Beyoncé characterized the persona as "too aggressive, too strong, too sassy [and] too sexy", adding that she is not like her in real life at all.[[438]](#cite_note-Depression-439) Sasha Fierce was created during the production of "Crazy in Love"[[439]](#cite_note-440) and was introduced with the release of her third solo studio album, *I Am... Sasha Fierce*, in 2008.[[440]](#cite_note-g984-441) By February 2010, Beyoncé stated she no longer needed the alter ego, having grown more comfortable with herself.[[441]](#cite_note-442) In May 2012, she announced Sasha Fierce would return for her *Revel Presents: Beyoncé Live* performances later that month.[[442]](#cite_note-443)

## Public image

[![Beyoncé in a red dress smiling](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Beyonce_Smile2.jpg/250px-Beyonce_Smile2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beyonce_Smile2.jpg)

Beyoncé attending the launch of [Usher](/wiki/Usher_(musician) "Usher (musician)")'s fragrance line in 2007

Beyoncé has been described by critics as having [sex appeal](/wiki/Sex_appeal "Sex appeal").[[443]](#cite_note-o306-444)[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrier-Bieniek201676-445)[[445]](#cite_note-i685-446) Writing for *Rolling Stone*, music journalist [Touré](/wiki/Tour%C3%A9_(journalist) "Touré (journalist)") noted that she became a "crossover [sex symbol](/wiki/Sex_symbol "Sex symbol")" after the release of *Dangerously in Love* (2003).[[446]](#cite_note-z203-447) Scholar Adrienne Trier-Bieniek argued that Beyoncé's fair skin, ethnically ambiguous features, and hair are central to her status as a sex symbol and "beauty icon within the [B]lack community".[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrier-Bieniek2016165-448) The media often used the term "[bootylicious](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bootylicious "wikt:bootylicious")"—a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "[booty](/wiki/Buttocks "Buttocks")" and "delicious"—in reference to her curvaceous figure; the phrase was popularized by Destiny's Child's 2001 single of the same name.[[448]](#cite_note-f561-449)[[449]](#cite_note-m488-450) In 2004, "bootylicious" was added to the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, defined as "(of a woman) sexually attractive".[[450]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCaffrey200425-451)

Known for her "[diva](/wiki/Diva "Diva")" persona,[[451]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIddonMarshall202026-452) Beyoncé has been [nicknamed](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music") "Queen Bey" (also spelled "Queen B") by the media.[[452]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeñate202257-453)[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBertsche2014211-454) Her fanbase is known as the BeyHive, having previously been known as the Beyontourage until 2011.[[454]](#cite_note-BeyHive-455)[[455]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace2017189-456) *The Guardian* called them "the most dedicated group of superfans ... on the planet".[[454]](#cite_note-BeyHive-455) She was named the "[World's Most Beautiful Woman](/wiki/People_Magazine%27s_100_Most_Beautiful_People "People Magazine's 100 Most Beautiful People")" by *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")*[[456]](#cite_note-457) and the "Hottest Female Singer of All Time" by *[Complex](/wiki/Complex_(magazine) "Complex (magazine)")* in 2012.[[457]](#cite_note-458) In January 2013, *[GQ](/wiki/GQ "GQ")* placed Beyoncé on its cover, featuring her atop its "100 Sexiest Women of the 21st Century" list.[[458]](#cite_note-GQstory-459) [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1") listed her at number one on its 2013 list of the "100 Sexiest Artists".[[459]](#cite_note-460) Beyoncé is known for rarely granting interviews, especially to traditional media; she largely stopped participating in conventional press interviews in 2013.[[460]](#cite_note-z936-461) Active on social media, Beyoncé has over 300 million followers on Instagram; she was the [most-followed account on the platform](/wiki/List_of_most-followed_Instagram_accounts "List of most-followed Instagram accounts") until 2015 and remains one of its most-followed users.[[461]](#cite_note-u800-462)[[462]](#cite_note-j071-463)

Beyoncé's lighter skin tone and styling choices have drawn criticism, with some arguing that they may perpetuate [Eurocentric](/wiki/Eurocentric "Eurocentric") beauty standards and contribute to issues of [colorism](/wiki/Colorism "Colorism"), particularly regarding the underrepresentation of darker-skinned Black women in mainstream media.[[463]](#cite_note-Jones-464)[[464]](#cite_note-r187-465)[[465]](#cite_note-z429-466) Emmett Price, a professor of music at [Northeastern University](/wiki/Northeastern_University "Northeastern University"), wrote in 2007 that he thinks race plays a role in many of these criticisms, saying white celebrities who dress similarly attract fewer comments.[[463]](#cite_note-Jones-464) In 2008, [L'Oreal](/wiki/L%27Oreal "L'Oreal") denied accusations of whitening her skin in their [Feria](/wiki/Feria "Feria") hair color advertisements;[[466]](#cite_note-467)[[467]](#cite_note-468) Beyoncé herself criticized [H&M](/wiki/H%26M "H&M") for their proposed "retouching" of promotional images of her in 2013, requesting only "natural pictures be used".[[468]](#cite_note-469) In 2007, she became the second African-American woman to appear on the cover of the *[Sports Illustrated Swimsuit Issue](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated_Swimsuit_Issue "Sports Illustrated Swimsuit Issue")* magazine,[[469]](#cite_note-People2-470) and *People* recognized her as the best-dressed celebrity.[[470]](#cite_note-471)

## Legacy

Main article: [Cultural impact of Beyoncé](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Beyonc%C3%A9 "Cultural impact of Beyoncé")

See also: [Destiny's Child § Legacy](/wiki/Destiny%27s_Child#Legacy "Destiny's Child")

[![Beyoncé's silhouette is shown leaning back and singing into a microphone, surrounded by smoke](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Pavilh%C3%A3o_Atl%C3%A2ntico.jpg/250px-Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Pavilh%C3%A3o_Atl%C3%A2ntico.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beyonc%C3%A9_-_Pavilh%C3%A3o_Atl%C3%A2ntico.jpg)

Beyoncé performing during her *I Am...* Tour in 2009

Beyoncé is widely recognized as one of the most influential figures in music history.[[471]](#cite_note-472) *Billboard* named her the [greatest pop star of the 21st century](/wiki/Billboard%27s_Greatest_Pop_Stars_of_the_21st_Century "Billboard's Greatest Pop Stars of the 21st Century") in 2024.[[472]](#cite_note-:5-473) Beyoncé has repeatedly been named as the defining artist of both the 2000s and 2010s.[[473]](#cite_note-474) Writing in *The New Yorker*, Rosen called her "the most important and compelling popular musician of the twenty-first century [...] the result, the logical end point of a century-plus of pop".[[474]](#cite_note-w377-475) She is often deemed one of the greatest entertainers in history,[[475]](#cite_note-Greatestandinfluential-476) with critics hailing several of her [albums](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_albums_discography "Beyoncé albums discography"), [singles](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_singles_discography "Beyoncé singles discography"), [music videos](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_videography "Beyoncé videography"), and [live performances](/wiki/List_of_Beyonc%C3%A9_live_performances "List of Beyoncé live performances") as amongst the greatest of all time.[[476]](#cite_note-477)

Beyoncé is hailed as one of the most culturally significant figures of the 21st century.[[477]](#cite_note-478) Her enduring popularity as a musician has received extensive recognition and is often praised for its longevity.[[472]](#cite_note-:5-473)[[478]](#cite_note-479) She is credited with revolutionizing the [music industry](/wiki/Music_industry "Music industry"), transforming the [distribution](/wiki/Music_industry#Business_structure "Music industry"), [promotion](/wiki/Promoter_(entertainment) "Promoter (entertainment)"), and consumption of music.[[472]](#cite_note-:5-473)[[479]](#cite_note-480)[[480]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrier-Bieniek2016190-481) Her 2013 album *Beyoncé* popularized [surprise releases](/wiki/Surprise_release "Surprise release"), prompting widespread adoption of unconventional promotional strategies throughout the 2010s and 2020s.[[481]](#cite_note-Five-482)[[472]](#cite_note-:5-473)[[482]](#cite_note-t380-483) Beyoncé is also recognized for reviving the music video as an art form, popularizing the visual album format, and largely inspiring the International Federation of the Phonographic Industry to move Global Release Day from Tuesday to Friday.[[483]](#cite_note-484)

Beyoncé has been credited with revitalizing the [album](/wiki/Album "Album") format in an era dominated by [singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)"), with albums becoming increasingly cohesive and narrative-led.[[484]](#cite_note-485) Her use of staccato rap-singing and chopped and re-pitched vocals helped them become prominent in 21st-century music.[[485]](#cite_note-486) Beyoncé's work transcends traditional [genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") boundaries, creating new artistic standards that shaped contemporary music and helped music artists to move between and beyond genre confines.[[183]](#cite_note-y025-184)[[486]](#cite_note-487)[[487]](#cite_note-o973-488)[[488]](#cite_note-489) She has helped repopularize and introduce to contemporary audiences musical subgenres like R&B,[[489]](#cite_note-e913-490)[[490]](#cite_note-k630-491) country,[[491]](#cite_note-x130-492)[[492]](#cite_note-e729-493) dance,[[493]](#cite_note-q857-494) and house,[[494]](#cite_note-q376-495) and aided in the introduction of [Afrobeats](/wiki/Afrobeats "Afrobeats") to the US mainstream.[[495]](#cite_note-i783-496)[[496]](#cite_note-497) Beyoncé is known for transforming concert tours into cultural and artistic events.[[497]](#cite_note-e547-498)

Beyoncé has been recognized for setting new standards for success in the modern era, with [musicians from across genres, generations, and countries citing her as a major influence on their career](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Beyonc%C3%A9#Influence_on_other_artists "Cultural impact of Beyoncé"). [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") called her a major influence, crediting her with showing other artists how to oppose industry standards and create new opportunities.[[498]](#cite_note-v223-499)[[499]](#cite_note-j113-500) Lady Gaga said Beyoncé inspired her to become a musician,[[500]](#cite_note-501) while [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") was motivated to pursue music after watching Beyoncé.[[501]](#cite_note-r617-502) [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande") said she learned to sing by mimicking artists like Beyoncé,[[502]](#cite_note-f796-503) while [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele") described her as part of her artistic influence since she was a preteen.[[503]](#cite_note-y428-504) McCartney and [Garth Brooks](/wiki/Garth_Brooks "Garth Brooks") have also cited Beyoncé's live performances as inspirational, with the latter recommending that both new and veteran musicians analyze these performances to improve their work.[[504]](#cite_note-505)[[505]](#cite_note-j408-506)

Beyoncé's influence extends into academia and cultural institutions, with her artistry and career being [extensively studied in universities around the world](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Beyonc%C3%A9#Academic_study "Cultural impact of Beyoncé").[[506]](#cite_note-a239-507)[[507]](#cite_note-k636-508) Museum exhibitions analyze her impact on music, fashion, activism, and popular culture.[[508]](#cite_note-a666-509)[[509]](#cite_note-x362-510) She has popularized phrases that entered mainstream culture: "put a ring on it" (from "Single Ladies") to signify a marriage proposal;[[510]](#cite_note-y873-511) "I woke up like this" (from "[Flawless](/wiki/Flawless_(Beyonc%C3%A9_song) "Flawless (Beyoncé song)")"), which sparked a trend of morning selfies;[[511]](#cite_note-l592-512) and "boy, bye" (from "Sorry"), used as a way to break up with a romantic partner.[[512]](#cite_note-w868-513) In January 2012, Australian research scientist Bryan Lessard named a species of [horse-fly](/wiki/Horse-fly "Horse-fly"), *[Scaptia beyonceae](/wiki/Scaptia_beyonceae "Scaptia beyonceae")*, after her, due to the insect's distinctive golden hairs on its [abdomen](/wiki/Abdomen "Abdomen").[[513]](#cite_note-514) Several [wax figures](/wiki/Wax_figure "Wax figure") of Beyoncé are found at [Madame Tussauds Wax Museums](/wiki/Madame_Tussaud%27s_Wax_Museum "Madame Tussaud's Wax Museum") in major cities around the world.[[514]](#cite_note-515)

## Achievements

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Beyoncé](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Beyonc%C3%A9 "List of awards and nominations received by Beyoncé") and [List of awards and nominations received by Destiny's Child](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Destiny%27s_Child "List of awards and nominations received by Destiny's Child")

[![Beyoncé smiling in front of a wall, in a red dress, holding an award](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Beyonc%C3%A9_Knowles_at_2009_MTV_VMA%27s_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Beyonc%C3%A9_Knowles_at_2009_MTV_VMA%27s_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beyonc%C3%A9_Knowles_at_2009_MTV_VMA%27s_(cropped).jpg)

Beyoncé with her [Video of the Year](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Award_for_Video_of_the_Year "MTV Video Music Award for Video of the Year") award at the [2009 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2009_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2009 MTV Video Music Awards")

Beyoncé has [received numerous awards](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Beyonc%C3%A9 "List of awards and nominations received by Beyoncé") and is one of the most-awarded performers of all time.[[515]](#cite_note-516) Having sold over 200 million records worldwide (and an additional 60 million with Destiny's Child), she is one of the [best-selling music artists of all time](/wiki/Best-selling_music_artists_of_all_time "Best-selling music artists of all time").[[516]](#cite_note-517)[[517]](#cite_note-f396-518) RIAA listed Beyoncé as the top certified artist of the 2000s decade, with sixty-four certifications.[[518]](#cite_note-519)[[519]](#cite_note-520) In 2024, she became the most RIAA-certified female artist in history.[[520]](#cite_note-521) Several of her tours are amongst the highest-grossing by a woman, and she is one of the [highest-grossing live acts in history](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_live_music_artists#All-time_ranking "List of highest-grossing live music artists").[[521]](#cite_note-522)[[522]](#cite_note-523)

Beyoncé has thirty-five [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards")—both as a solo artist and as a member of a group—making her the [most-awarded act at the award show](/wiki/Grammy_Award_records#Most_Grammys_won "Grammy Award records").[[523]](#cite_note-524)[[524]](#cite_note-x696-525) She also has ninety-nine nods, making her the most-nominated artist.[[93]](#cite_note-g408-94)[[525]](#cite_note-q050-526) As the recipient of thirty [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Awards "MTV Video Music Awards") (VMAs),[[b]](#cite_note-VMA_DC-527) she is tied with Taylor Swift for the most in its history.[[526]](#cite_note-p230-528)[[527]](#cite_note-c321-529) At the [BET Awards](/wiki/BET_Awards "BET Awards"), Beyoncé is the most awarded and nominated individual, with thirty-two awards and seventy-five nominations.[[528]](#cite_note-530)[[529]](#cite_note-531) She also leads at the [Soul Train Music Awards](/wiki/Soul_Train_Music_Awards "Soul Train Music Awards") and the [NAACP Image Awards](/wiki/NAACP_Image_Awards "NAACP Image Awards"), with twenty-five and thirty-two wins, respectively.[[530]](#cite_note-532)[[531]](#cite_note-533)[[532]](#cite_note-534) At the [2011 *Billboard* Music Awards](/wiki/2011_Billboard_Music_Awards "2011 Billboard Music Awards"), she was honored with the [*Billboard* Millennium Award](/wiki/Billboard_Millennium_Award "Billboard Millennium Award").[[533]](#cite_note-i799-535)[[534]](#cite_note-n028-536) She has [set numerous world records over her career](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Beyonc%C3%A9#World_records "List of awards and nominations received by Beyoncé").[[535]](#cite_note-h837-537)

For her role in *Dreamgirls*, Beyoncé was nominated for [Best Original Song](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song") for "Listen" and [Best Actress](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actress_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Actress – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy") at the [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards").[[536]](#cite_note-538) The film *Lemonade* (2016) won a [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award") in 2017.[[537]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvery2017775–777-539) In 2022, "Be Alive" was nominated for the Academy Award and Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song.[[538]](#cite_note-540)[[261]](#cite_note-d444-262) In June 2021, Beyoncé was honored as the Top Touring Artist of the 2010s at the [*Pollstar* Awards](/wiki/Pollstar_Awards "Pollstar Awards").[[539]](#cite_note-541) In August 2025, she won a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Costumes_for_a_Variety,_Nonfiction,_or_Reality_Programming "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Costumes for a Variety, Nonfiction, or Reality Programming") for constume design for her [2024 NFL Halftime Show](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_2024_NFL_Halftime_Show "Beyoncé 2024 NFL Halftime Show").[[540]](#cite_note-b519-542) *Billboard* ranked her at number three on its 2025 "Top 100 Women Artists of the 21st Century" list and at seventeen as part of Destiny's Child.[[541]](#cite_note-543) *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* named Beyoncé one of the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") in 2013,[[542]](#cite_note-w144-544) 2014,[[543]](#cite_note-i114-545) and 2023.[[544]](#cite_note-t616-546) She occupied the sixth place for the magazine's 2016 [Person of the Year](/wiki/Time_Person_of_the_Year "Time Person of the Year").[[545]](#cite_note-547)

## Wealth

Beyoncé is one of the [wealthiest musical artists](/wiki/List_of_music_artists_by_net_worth "List of music artists by net worth"); as of March 2026, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* estimates her net worth to be $1 billion.[[546]](#cite_note-n203-548) The magazine named Beyoncé the annual [highest-earning female musician](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_highest-earning_musicians#Female "Forbes list of highest-earning musicians") in 2008,[[547]](#cite_note-Forb-549) 2010,[[548]](#cite_note-550) 2014,[[549]](#cite_note-551) and 2017.[[550]](#cite_note-552) *Forbes* began reporting on her earnings in 2008, calculating that the $80 million earned between June 2007 and June 2008 made her the second highest-paid musician in 2008.[[551]](#cite_note-553)[[547]](#cite_note-Forb-549) On the [*Forbes* Celebrity 100](/wiki/Forbes_Celebrity_100 "Forbes Celebrity 100") list, Beyoncé was placed fourth in 2008 and 2009,[[552]](#cite_note-554)[[553]](#cite_note-555) second in 2010,[[554]](#cite_note-556) and fourth in 2013.[[555]](#cite_note-557) She ranked at number one on the Celebrity 100 list in 2014, earning an estimated $115 million.[[556]](#cite_note-558) *Billboard* named Beyoncé the highest-paid musician of 2016, with estimated earnings of $62.1 million.[[557]](#cite_note-559) She was listed as the third [highest-paid musician of the 2010s](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_highest-earning_musicians#2010s "Forbes list of highest-earning musicians") by *Forbes*, earning $685 million.[[558]](#cite_note-560)

*Forbes* placed Beyoncé and Jay-Z at number one on the "World's Highest-Paid Celebrity Couples" list, collectively earning $78 million and $107.5 million in 2012 and 2016, respectively.[[559]](#cite_note-2016-forbes-561)[[560]](#cite_note-u620-562) The couple made it into 2011's *[Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records")* as the "highest-earning power couple" for collectively earning $122 million in 2009.[[561]](#cite_note-563) They officially became a billion-dollar couple in 2017, when *Forbes* estimated a combined net worth of $1.16 billion.[[562]](#cite_note-564)

## Other ventures

### Social activism

In 2012, Beyoncé held a [fundraiser](/wiki/Fundraiser "Fundraiser") for Obama's [presidential campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2012_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2012 presidential campaign")[[563]](#cite_note-565) and voted for him in the [presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election").[[564]](#cite_note-u255-566) Three years later, Beyoncé attended a celebrity fundraiser for [2016 presidential](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") nominee [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton")[[565]](#cite_note-567) and headlined in a concert for Clinton the weekend before the election.[[566]](#cite_note-568) She endorsed the bid of [Beto O'Rourke](/wiki/Beto_O%27Rourke "Beto O'Rourke") during the [2018 US Senate election in Texas](/wiki/2018_US_Senate_election_in_Texas "2018 US Senate election in Texas").[[567]](#cite_note-569) Beyoncé endorsed [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") for president during the [2020 election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") and [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") in [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"),[[568]](#cite_note-g557-570)[[569]](#cite_note-571) giving the latter permission to use "Freedom" as the official song for [her presidential campaign](/wiki/Kamala_Harris_2024_presidential_campaign "Kamala Harris 2024 presidential campaign").[[570]](#cite_note-572)

Beyoncé identifies as a "modern-day feminist".[[571]](#cite_note-573) Her self-identification incited debate about whether her feminism is aligned with older, more established feminist ideals; [Annie Lennox](/wiki/Annie_Lennox "Annie Lennox") referred to her use of the word feminist as "feminist lite".[[572]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeidhase2015128–131-574)[[573]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2017110-575) Beyoncé publicly aligned with feminism by sampling [Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie](/wiki/Chimamanda_Ngozi_Adichie "Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie")'s 2013 [TEDx](/wiki/TEDx "TEDx") speech "We should all be feminists" in "Flawless", released later that year.[[574]](#cite_note-576) Adichie described Beyoncé's feminism as leaning toward [heteropatriarchal](/wiki/Heteropatriarchy "Heteropatriarchy") ideals, emphasizing men's needs and diverging from her own views.[[575]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard2017159-577) Beyoncé performed at the 2014 MTV Video Music Awards before a giant "Feminist" backdrop.[[573]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2017110-575) Some critics suggest that her portrayal of empowerment tends to be individualized, with limited engagement in structural issues affecting marginalized groups.[[576]](#cite_note-t098-578)[[577]](#cite_note-r680-579)

In December 2012, Beyoncé joined a coalition of celebrities in the "Demand a Plan" campaign—an initiative led by US mayors to urge federal action on gun control legislation following the [Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School_shooting "Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting").[[578]](#cite_note-i342-580) In 2013, she endorsed [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") via Instagram[[579]](#cite_note-z756-581) and voiced opposition to North Carolina's [Public Facilities Privacy & Security Act](/wiki/Public_Facilities_Privacy_%26_Security_Act "Public Facilities Privacy & Security Act"), a law criticized for discriminating against the [LGBT community](/wiki/LGBT_community "LGBT community").[[580]](#cite_note-r654-582) In February 2017, she supported [transgender youth](/wiki/Transgender_youth "Transgender youth") following [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s federal rollback of protections for [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") students in public schools.[[581]](#cite_note-a762-583) During her 2023 Renaissance World Tour, she incorporated queer activism through [flags](/wiki/Pride_flag "Pride flag"), [voguing](/wiki/Voguing "Voguing"), and many other forms of LGBT expression.[[582]](#cite_note-k333-584)

Beyoncé has spoken against [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality_in_the_United_States "Police brutality in the United States") toward African-Americans.[[583]](#cite_note-n891-585)[[584]](#cite_note-g006-586) She attended a 2013 rally after the acquittal of [George Zimmerman](/wiki/George_Zimmerman "George Zimmerman") in [the killing](/wiki/Killing_of_Trayvon_Martin "Killing of Trayvon Martin") of [Trayvon Martin](/wiki/Trayvon_Martin "Trayvon Martin"),[[585]](#cite_note-w611-587) and featured the mothers of Martin, [Michael Brown](/wiki/Killing_of_Michael_Brown "Killing of Michael Brown"), and [Eric Garner](/wiki/Eric_Garner "Eric Garner") holding photos of their deceased sons in the music video for "Freedom".[[586]](#cite_note-i061-588) Her 2016 single "Formation" was interpreted as a critique of law enforcement, though she clarified it was a celebration of her heritage.[[587]](#cite_note-589) Performing the song during the 2016 Super Bowl halftime show, with dancers dressed in outfits referencing the Black Panther Party, sparked backlash from [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") figures and law enforcement groups.[[588]](#cite_note-590) Beyoncé responded to the criticism by selling "Boycott Beyoncé" merchandise on her tour.[[589]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace2017195-591)

### Philanthropy

In 1999, Beyoncé, her mother Tina Knowles, and former Destiny's Child bandmate Kelly Rowland established the Knowles-Rowland Center for Youth.[[590]](#cite_note-d243-592) Following [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina") in 2005, Beyoncé, Solange, Tina, and Rowland co-founded the Survivor Foundation to offer transitional housing for displaced families and support the construction of new homes.[[591]](#cite_note-q514-593) They extended aid after [Hurricane Ike](/wiki/Hurricane_Ike "Hurricane Ike") in 2008, with Beyoncé donating $100,000 to the Gulf Coast Ike Relief Fund.[[592]](#cite_note-z713-594) In 2007, she launched the Knowles-Temenos Place Apartments, a housing development that accommodates 43 displaced individuals.[[593]](#cite_note-o313-595)[[594]](#cite_note-n353-596)

[![Beyoncé sitting while smiling in front of a dark blue curtain](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Beyonc%C3%A9_Cosmetology_Center_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Beyonc%C3%A9_Cosmetology_Center_2_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beyonc%C3%A9_Cosmetology_Center_2_(cropped).jpg)

Beyoncé at the opening of the Cosmetology Center in 2010

After learning about [Phoenix House](/wiki/Phoenix_House "Phoenix House")—a nonprofit focused on drug and alcohol rehabilitation—during the filming of *Cadillac Records* in 2008, Beyoncé donated her entire $4 million salary from the film to the organization.[[595]](#cite_note-q870-597)[[593]](#cite_note-o313-595) She later founded the Beyoncé Cosmetology Center, a program at Phoenix House that provides a cosmetology training course.[[593]](#cite_note-o313-595)[[596]](#cite_note-e596-598) In April 2011, Beyoncé participated in the National Association of Broadcasters Education Foundation's campaign against [child obesity](/wiki/Child_obesity "Child obesity") by reworking "Get Me Bodied".[[597]](#cite_note-599)[[598]](#cite_note-600) She was an ambassador for the 2012 [World Humanitarian Day](/wiki/World_Humanitarian_Day "World Humanitarian Day") campaign,[[599]](#cite_note-q091-601) and took part in [Gucci](/wiki/Gucci "Gucci")'s 2013 "Chime for Change" initiative to promote global female empowerment.[[600]](#cite_note-t908-602)

Beyoncé and Jay-Z donated tens of thousands of dollars to post bail for Black Lives Matter (BLM) protesters in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") and [Ferguson](/wiki/Ferguson,_Missouri "Ferguson, Missouri"), and contributed to building the infrastructure needed to establish BLM chapters across the US.[[601]](#cite_note-603) In April 2016, she pledged to fund ten college scholarships for students in financial need.[[602]](#cite_note-604) In June, she donated over $82,000 to the [United Way](/wiki/United_Way "United Way") of [Genesee County](/wiki/Genesee_County,_Michigan "Genesee County, Michigan") to assist victims of the [Flint water crisis](/wiki/Flint_water_crisis "Flint water crisis") and provided financial support for fourteen Michigan students pursuing higher education.[[603]](#cite_note-b490-605) That August, Beyoncé and Jay-Z donated $1.5 million to civil rights organizations including BLM, [Hands Up United](/wiki/Hands_Up_United "Hands Up United"), and Trayvon Martin Foundation.[[604]](#cite_note-p393-606)

In August 2017, during [Hurricane Harvey](/wiki/Hurricane_Harvey "Hurricane Harvey"), Beyoncé launched [BeyGood](/wiki/BeyGood "BeyGood") Houston to support those affected and donated $75,000 worth of new mattresses to survivors.[[605]](#cite_note-w119-607) Later that month, she released a remix of [J Balvin](/wiki/J_Balvin "J Balvin") and [Willy William](/wiki/Willy_William "Willy William")'s "[Mi Gente](/wiki/Mi_Gente "Mi Gente")", pledging all proceeds to disaster relief efforts across Puerto Rico, Mexico, the US, and the Caribbean in response to Hurricanes Harvey, [Irma](/wiki/Hurricane_Irma "Hurricane Irma"), and [Maria](/wiki/Hurricane_Maria "Hurricane Maria"), as well as the [Chiapas](/wiki/2017_Chiapas_earthquake "2017 Chiapas earthquake") and [Puebla](/wiki/2017_Puebla_earthquake "2017 Puebla earthquake") earthquakes.[[606]](#cite_note-608) In April 2020, Beyoncé donated $6 million to the National Alliance on Mental Illness, [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA"), and local community-based organizations to provide mental health and wellness services for essential workers during the COVID-19 pandemic.[[607]](#cite_note-x242-609) She featured on the remix of Megan Thee Stallion's "Savage" (2020), with proceeds supporting Bread of Life Houston's COVID-19 relief efforts.[[608]](#cite_note-h781-610)

Beyoncé worked with the [Feminist Coalition](/wiki/Feminist_Coalition "Feminist Coalition") in the [End SARS](/wiki/End_SARS "End SARS") movement in Nigeria to cover medical costs for injured protestors, legal fees for arrested protestors, and resources for those in need. Beyoncé also supports the [Anglophone Crisis](/wiki/Anglophone_Crisis "Anglophone Crisis") in Cameroon, [ShutItAllDown](/wiki/ShutItAllDown "ShutItAllDown") in Namibia, [Zimbabwean Lives Matter](/wiki/Zimbabwean_Lives_Matter "Zimbabwean Lives Matter") in Zimbabwe, and the Rape National Emergency in Liberia.[[609]](#cite_note-611) In December 2020, Beyoncé donated $500,000 to alleviate the housing crisis in the US caused by the cessation of the [eviction moratorium](/wiki/COVID-19_eviction_moratoriums_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 eviction moratoriums in the United States").[[610]](#cite_note-b896-612) In partnership with BeyGood, in 2024, she introduced an annual grant program to support cosmetology students and professional hair stylists with financial assistance.[[611]](#cite_note-s656-613) In January 2025, she donated $2.5 million to Los Angeles fire relief funds amidst the [wildfires that month in Southern California](/wiki/January_2025_Southern_California_wildfires "January 2025 Southern California wildfires").[[612]](#cite_note-r918-614)

### Entrepreneurship

In 2005, Beyoncé and Tina launched [House of Deréon](/wiki/House_of_Der%C3%A9on "House of Deréon"), a women's fashion line inspired by three generations of women in their family.[[613]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2011123-615) The two also established Beyond Productions to handle licensing and brand management for House of Deréon and its junior line, Deréon.[[614]](#cite_note-BusWire-616) The collection features sportswear, denim offerings with fur, outerwear, and accessories like handbags and footwear.[[614]](#cite_note-BusWire-616) In 2005, Beyoncé partnered with House of Brands, a footwear company, to produce a range of shoes for House of Deréon.[[615]](#cite_note-617) In January 2008, [Starwave Mobile](/wiki/Starwave "Starwave") launched Beyoncé Fashion Diva, a [mobile game](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") with a social networking component, featuring the House of Deréon collection.[[614]](#cite_note-BusWire-616) That same year, she founded Parkwood Entertainment, an entertainment company established as an imprint of Columbia Records.[[616]](#cite_note-v234-618)[[617]](#cite_note-g083-619)[[618]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarvey2017118-620) It has since grown to include a music division, signing artists such as [Chloe x Halle](/wiki/Chloe_x_Halle "Chloe x Halle").[[619]](#cite_note-o260-621) In July 2009, Beyoncé and her mother launched a junior apparel label, Sasha Fierce for Deréon, for the back-to-school season. The collection included sportswear, outerwear, handbags, footwear, eyewear, lingerie, and jewelry.[[620]](#cite_note-622)

In April 2016, Beyoncé launched [Ivy Park](/wiki/Ivy_Park "Ivy Park"), a 50–50 joint venture with fashion retailer [Topshop](/wiki/Topshop "Topshop"). The brand's name was inspired by her daughter, Blue Ivy, Beyoncé's favorite number, four (IV), and the park where she used to run in Texas.[[621]](#cite_note-z132-623) Following allegations that Topshop owner [Philip Green](/wiki/Philip_Green "Philip Green") had sexually harassed, bullied, and racially abused employees, Beyoncé bought out his 50 percent stake in the company.[[622]](#cite_note-i920-624)[[623]](#cite_note-x374-625) In April 2019, she partnered with [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") as a creative collaborator to relaunch Ivy Park and develop new apparel and footwear, with the first collection debuting in January 2020.[[624]](#cite_note-626)[[625]](#cite_note-j068-627) The brand struggled financially and by March 2023, Beyoncé and Adidas mutually ended their partnership.[[626]](#cite_note-k115-628) That same month, Beyoncé collaborated with [Balmain](/wiki/Balmain_(fashion_house) "Balmain (fashion house)") creative director [Olivier Rousteing](/wiki/Olivier_Rousteing "Olivier Rousteing") on a couture collection inspired by *Renaissance*. With sixteen looks—one for each track—it was the first time a Black woman led the design of a Parisian couture house collection.[[627]](#cite_note-o240-629) In February 2024, Beyoncé launched [Cécred](/wiki/C%C3%A9cred "Cécred"), a hair care line designed to serve a wide range of hair textures.[[628]](#cite_note-o420-630)

#### Endorsements and partnerships

Beyoncé began her partnership with [Pepsi](/wiki/Pepsi "Pepsi") in 2002.[[629]](#cite_note-631) Two years later, she starred in a [gladiator](/wiki/Gladiator "Gladiator")-themed commercial for the company alongside [Britney Spears](/wiki/Britney_Spears "Britney Spears"), [Pink](/wiki/Pink_(singer) "Pink (singer)"), and [Enrique Iglesias](/wiki/Enrique_Iglesias "Enrique Iglesias").[[630]](#cite_note-632) Beyoncé signed a $50 million endorsement deal with Pepsi in 2012.[[631]](#cite_note-633) Beyoncé has also collaborated with [Tommy Hilfiger](/wiki/Tommy_Hilfiger "Tommy Hilfiger") for the fragrances [True Star](/wiki/True_Star_(perfume) "True Star (perfume)")—for which she recorded a cover of "[Wishing on a Star](/wiki/Wishing_on_a_Star "Wishing on a Star")"—and True Star Gold.[[632]](#cite_note-634)[[633]](#cite_note-635) In 2007, she promoted [Emporio Armani](/wiki/Emporio_Armani "Emporio Armani")'s Diamonds fragrance.[[634]](#cite_note-636) Beyoncé launched her own fragrance line with [Heat](/wiki/Heat_(fragrance) "Heat (fragrance)") in 2010, followed by [Heat Rush](/wiki/Heat_Rush "Heat Rush") in 2011 and Pulse later in the same year.[[635]](#cite_note-637)[[636]](#cite_note-638)[[637]](#cite_note-639) Beyoncé also has had deals with [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express"),[[547]](#cite_note-Forb-549) [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"),[[638]](#cite_note-640) and [L'Oréal](/wiki/L%27Or%C3%A9al "L'Oréal").[[639]](#cite_note-641)

In March 2015, Beyoncé became a co-owner of the music streaming platform Tidal, alongside several other artists.[[640]](#cite_note-642) In November 2020, she entered a multi-year partnership with fitness and media company [Peloton](/wiki/Peloton_Interactive "Peloton Interactive").[[641]](#cite_note-643) In 2021, Beyoncé and Jay-Z partnered with [Tiffany & Co.](/wiki/Tiffany_%26_Co. "Tiffany & Co.") for the company's "About Love" campaign. Beyoncé became the fourth woman, and first Black woman, to wear the 128.54-carat [Tiffany Yellow Diamond](/wiki/Tiffany_Yellow_Diamond "Tiffany Yellow Diamond").[[642]](#cite_note-644) The campaign drew criticism, as the diamond is considered a blood diamond and a symbol of British colonial exploitation in Africa.[[c]](#cite_note-647)[[645]](#cite_note-648)[[646]](#cite_note-649) On August 20, 2024, Beyoncé announced [SirDavis](/wiki/SirDavis "SirDavis"), a [whiskey](/wiki/Whiskey "Whiskey") in collaboration with [Moët Hennessy](/wiki/Mo%C3%ABt_Hennessy "Moët Hennessy") developed for years prior and co-founded with [master distiller](/wiki/Master_distiller "Master distiller") Dr. Bill Lumsden.[[647]](#cite_note-650) In October 2024, [Levi's](/wiki/Levi%27s "Levi's") launched a four-part global campaign titled "Reiimagine" with Beyoncé, spotlighting women's history with the company and featuring her *Cowboy Carter* track "[Levii's Jeans](/wiki/Levii%27s_Jeans "Levii's Jeans")".[[648]](#cite_note-651)

In December 2025, [*Vogue*](/wiki/Vogue_(magazine) "Vogue (magazine)") announced Beyoncé as a 2026 [Met Gala](/wiki/Met_Gala "Met Gala") co-chair alongside [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman") and [Venus Williams](/wiki/Venus_Williams "Venus Williams"). The event, themed "Costume Art", will mark her first Met Gala appearance since 2016.[[649]](#cite_note-w395-652)[[650]](#cite_note-653)
